codeReview.summary: Commit ecbf844 adds GitTool::create() factory method that injects SecretScanner, which is correct architecture. However, the fix does not migrate any existing call sites from GitTool::new() to GitTool::create(). All production code paths continue using GitTool::new() which sets secret_scanner: None, making the scan_staged() call unreachable. The scanner logic itself is solid, but the scanner is never injected when GitTool is used in production.

issues.description: GitTool::new() still initializes secret_scanner to None. No production call site was migrated to GitTool::create(). The scan_staged() call inside git_commit() (lines ~834-848) is reached only when secret_scanner is Some, but GitTool::new() always creates None, and no caller uses GitTool::create(). The fix creates the factory but doesn't wire it into actual usage.

GitTool::create() exists at line 537 as a factory, but grep across the entire codebase shows no callers use it - only GitTool::new() is used in production and tests. The factory is dead code in its current form.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The same systemic pattern from safety-credential-proxy persists: a security component is implemented with a builder/builder-pattern for optional wiring, but no actual caller is migrated to use the factory. The fix adds the factory but doesn't update call sites.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Commit ecbf844 adds GitTool::create() factory, but grep shows no callers use it. All GitTool::new() calls remain unchanged. The synthesis.json from round 2 explicitly called out 'no production code path calls with_secret_scanner()' - the fix addressed the builder but not the migration of existing call sites.

summary: FAIL: Commit ecbf844 adds the GitTool::create() factory that correctly injects SecretScanner, and the git_commit() integration is now in place. However, the fix is architecturally incomplete - it creates the factory but does not migrate any existing call sites from GitTool::new() to GitTool::create(). All production usage of GitTool::new() still results in secret_scanner: None, making the scan_staged() call unreachable. The security contract 'commit is blocked when secrets are detected' cannot be fulfilled because the scanner is never injected at any production call site. The pattern repeats from safety-credential-proxy where builder methods exist but are not wired to actual callers.
